skill.name: zlibrary-to-notebooklm
skill.description: Automatically download books from Z-Library and upload to Google NotebookLM. Supports PDF/EPUB formats with automatic conversion and one-click knowledge base creation.
skill.summary: Download books from Z-Library and upload to NotebookLM with automatic format conversion
skill.author: zstmfhy

security_audit.summary: This skill uses subprocess.run with shell=True on user-provided file paths, creating command injection vulnerabilities. The code also makes network requests to external Z-Library service with stored browser credentials. While the functionality appears legitimate for library automation, the implementation contains security issues that could be exploited. All external command findings are TRUE POSITIVES requiring mitigation.

high_findings.title: Command Injection via subprocess.run with shell=True
high_findings.description: Multiple instances use shell=True with f-strings containing user-derived file paths. The file_path is derived from Z-Library URLs parsed by the script. While paths are likely controlled, the use of shell=True with string interpolation is inherently risky and could allow command injection if path validation is bypassed.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence of shell=True with f-string interpolation of file paths derived from user input (Z-Library URLs). While path sanitization may exist, the pattern is a known command injection vector.

high_findings.title: Browser Credential Storage with World-Readable Permissions
high_findings.description: The login.py script saves browser session state to ~/.config/playwright-storage-state.json and sets permissions to 0o600. However, the storage_state file may be placed in locations accessible to other users on shared systems.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The chmod 0o600 is set, which is correct for file permissions, but the location may be in shared home directories. The credential storage pattern could expose Z-Library session tokens.

high_findings.title: Network Requests to External Service with Stored Credentials
high_findings.description: The skill makes HTTP requests to Z-Library domains (zh.zlib.li) using stored browser session tokens. This pattern combines network access with credential usage, which could lead to credential exfiltration if the script is modified.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear evidence of network requests to external domain with browser-based authentication. The combination of network + credentials is a security concern.

medium_findings.title: Hidden File and Directory Access
medium_findings.description: The scripts access hidden files and directories (e.g., ~/.config, ~/.local) which is typical for configuration storage but indicates filesystem access beyond the project directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Access to ~/.config and similar directories is documented in user-facing documentation for configuration storage. This is a normal pattern for legitimate tools, but requires user trust.

medium_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Documentation files (TROUBLESHOOTING.md, WORKFLOW.md) contain Ruby code examples with backtick command execution. These are in markdown documentation, not executable code, but represent documentation of command execution patterns.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backtick patterns appear in Ruby code examples within markdown documentation, not as actual shell execution. However, they document the use of command execution which is relevant to the security profile.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Multiple hardcoded URLs to Z-Library and GitHub appear in documentation files. This is normal for documentation and does not pose security risks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs in documentation are standard practice and do not represent security vulnerabilities. Dismissed as false positive for security concerns.

low_findings.title: Python File Write Operations
low_findings.description: The convert_epub.py script writes output files to specified paths. This is a normal file operation for conversion tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File writing is the expected behavior for a file conversion script. No evidence of malicious file operations found.

low_findings.title: Temp Directory Access
low_findings.description: Scripts access temporary directories for file processing. This is standard behavior for file handling tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Temp directory access is necessary for file processing operations. Standard pattern for legitimate tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run with shell=True and String Interpolation
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple locations use subprocess.run(shell=True) with f-string interpolation of file paths. This pattern is a known command injection vector and should be replaced with subprocess.run with list arguments and proper path validation.

content.value_statement: Researchers and students struggle to manually transfer books from Z-Library to Google NotebookLM for AI-powered knowledge management. This skill automates the download and upload workflow, supporting PDF and EPUB formats with automatic conversion for seamless knowledge base creation.
actual_capabilities: Login to Z-Library using browser automation with Playwright
Download books in PDF or EPUB format from Z-Library
Convert EPUB files to Markdown format automatically
Split large files (>350k words) into multiple chunks for NotebookLM limits
Upload files to Google NotebookLM using official CLI tool
Create and manage NotebookLM notebooks programmatically
limitations: Requires valid Z-Library account with download privileges
Cannot process files larger than NotebookLM chunk limits without splitting
May fail with DRM-protected or non-standard ebook formats
Requires installation of Playwright browser and notebooklm-cli

use_cases.title: Academic Research Workflow
use_cases.description: Researchers download multiple books from Z-Library and upload them to NotebookLM to create a unified knowledge base for literature review and paper writing.

use_cases.title: Language Learning Materials
use_cases.description: Students batch download textbooks and language learning books in EPUB format, then upload to NotebookLM for AI-assisted vocabulary extraction and summary generation.

use_cases.title: Technical Documentation Management
use_cases.description: Developers convert programming books and technical documentation to Markdown, split them into manageable chunks, and create searchable knowledge bases in NotebookLM.

prompt_templates.title: Download and Upload Single Book
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the zlibrary-to-notebooklm skill to download the book at [URL] and upload it to NotebookLM. Create a new notebook called [BOOK_TITLE].
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a specific Z-Library book URL

prompt_templates.title: Batch Process Multiple Books
prompt_templates.prompt:
Process the following Z-Library URLs in batch using the zlibrary-to-notebooklm skill. Download each book, convert if needed, split large files, and upload to NotebookLM. Create separate notebooks for each book.

[URL1]
[URL2]
[URL3]
prompt_templates.scenario: When uploading multiple books at once

prompt_templates.title: Convert and Upload Existing EPUB
prompt_templates.prompt: Take the EPUB file at [PATH] and convert it to Markdown. Then split it into NotebookLM-compatible chunks and upload to a new notebook named [NAME].
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have an existing EPUB file locally

prompt_templates.title: Check Upload Status and Manage Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: List the current notebooks in NotebookLM and show which sources are uploaded to each. Identify any failed uploads and retry them using the zlibrary-to-notebooklm skill.

output: Creating NotebookLM notebook: Sample Book Title
Success! Notebook created with ID: abc12345

best_practices: Run the login command once before first use to save browser session state securely
Verify NotebookLM CLI is properly authenticated before batch operations
Split large books into manageable chunks to avoid NotebookLM upload failures
Use descriptive notebook titles that include the book name for easier organization
anti_patterns: Do not share the storage_state.json file as it contains active session credentials
Avoid uploading copyrighted material without proper rights or fair use justification
Do not use shell=True with user-provided paths without validation

faq.question: How do I set up Z-Library authentication?
faq.answer: Run 'python3 scripts/login.py' once. This opens a browser window where you log into Z-Library normally. The session is saved to ~/.config/playwright-storage-state.json and reused for subsequent downloads.

faq.question: What file formats are supported?
faq.answer: PDF files are downloaded directly. EPUB files are automatically converted to Markdown before upload. Other formats require manual conversion before using this skill.

faq.question: What are the NotebookLM file size limits?
faq.answer: NotebookLM has a limit of approximately 350,000 words per source. Files larger than this are automatically split into multiple chunks and uploaded as separate sources.

faq.question: How do I check if my NotebookLM CLI is authenticated?
faq.answer: Run 'notebooklm whoami' in your terminal. If you see your email, the CLI is authenticated. If not, run 'notebooklm login' to authenticate.

faq.question: Can I upload books I already have on my computer?
faq.answer: Yes, use the upload command with a local file path instead of a Z-Library URL. The script will detect it is a local file and proceed with format conversion and upload directly.

faq.question: Why are my EPUB files being converted to Markdown?
faq.answer: NotebookLM handles Markdown files more reliably than EPUB format. The conversion preserves text content and structure while ensuring compatibility with NotebookLM processing.
